Optimize metrics by up to 80%

Adaptive Metrics eliminates unused time series data through aggregation and cardinality optimization.

Case study: How The Trade Desk found $1.74M in savings with Adaptive Metrics

FAQ

What is metrics billable series?

Metrics billable series is calculated based on two components: active series and data points per minute (DPM). By understanding these components, youâll be able to better manage usage and reduce costs for your organization. See pricing docs –>

Do you provide discounts for higher usage volumes?

Yes, we provide volume discounts for enterprise-scale usage. Contact us for more details.

FAQ

How do you define process, write, retain?

Process: The work done to receive, process and optimize the signals sent by your systems.

Write: The work done to write only the useful data to the database.

Retain: The work done to retain and protect your stored data over time.

Do you offer retention greater than 30 days?

Yes, we have additional retention options available. Contact us for more details.

Do you offer discounts for higher usage?

Yes, we provide volume discounts for enterprise-scale usage. Contact us for more details.

FAQ

What exactly is a "Host Hour"?

A Host is any physical or virtual OS instance sending observability signals (including K8s metrics). It is considered active if it has sent a signal in the last 15 minutes.

Your bill is the sum of these active hours throughout the monthâmeaning you don’t pay for hosts that are spun down over the weekend or during off-peak hours.

When does host hour pricing apply?

Host hour billing applies when you opt into the curated experience built off the raw telemetry data.

Alternatively, you can opt out of the curated experience and be billed solely based on telemetry storage.

How is this different from included telemetry pricing model?

We made this change to give customers more control on telemetry usage and costs.

Customers can now use adaptive telemetry to reduce the unused / unwanted telemetry and lower their overall costs.

Read more in the documentation.

FAQ

How is this different from included telemetry pricing model?

We made this change to give customers more control on telemetry usage and costs. Customers can now use adaptive telemetry to reduce the unused / unwanted telemetry and lower their overall costs.

What is a session?

A “session” is defined as the time that a user is spending in a frontend application on and off from start to finish or timeout. A user session is either created or resumed when a user visits and uses the site. A user session ends after a maximum lifetime of 4 hours or after inactivity timeout of 15 minutes. The SDK will automatically create a new user session in any of these situations, if the user returns.

Do you provide discounts for higher usage volumes?

Yes, we provide volume discounts for enterprise-scale usage. Contact us for more details.

FAQ

What exactly is a "Host Hour" for Database Observability?

A database host is any database instance sending monitoring data to Grafana Cloud. It is considered active if it has sent data in the last 15 minutes.

Your bill is the sum of active host hours throughout the month, meaning you don’t pay for database instances that are spun down over the weekend or during off-peak hours.

What counts as a database host?

Each database server instance counts as one database host â for example, each MySQL or PostgreSQL server, each RDS or Cloud SQL instance, or each node in a database cluster. If you run multiple database instances on a single machine, each instance is counted separately.

Do you provide discounts for higher usage volumes?

Yes, we provide volume discounts for enterprise-scale usage. Contact us for more details.

What telemetry costs should I expect?

Database Observability generates metrics and logs that are stored in Grafana Cloud. These are billed at standard Grafana Cloud rates for metrics and logs, separate from your host hour usage. You can control telemetry volume by adjusting your collection configuration in Grafana Alloy.

FAQ

What is a test execution?

A test execution is a Synthetic test running in a probe location per minute of run time. Example: A synthetic test running in 5 locations that takes 1.5 minutes to complete will consume 10 executions each time it runs.

How do I estimate the number of test executions?

To estimate the number of test executions per month, you will need the following inputs:

  • Number of Locations (Probes)
  • Number of Targets (Tests)
  • Duration of Test (rounded to the nearest minute)
  • Frequency of Tests (in Minutes)
Using the above info, you can calculate the number of test executions per month by applying this formula (where 43,200 is the total minutes in a month): probes x tests x duration x (43,200 / frequency)

Do you provide discounts for higher usage volumes?

Yes, we provide volume discounts for enterprise-scale usage. Contact us for more details.

FAQ

What is a test execution?

A test execution is a Synthetic test running in a probe location per minute of run time. Example: A synthetic test running in 5 locations that takes 1.5 minutes to complete will consume 10 executions each time it runs.

What are MultiHTTP checks and k6 scripted checks?

MultiHTTP checks are like HTTP checks but you can test multiple URLs in a single check to validate multi-step user flows. k6 scripted checks are like MultiHTTP checks, but you can write the checks in code instead of via a form-based approach.

How do I estimate the number of test executions?

To estimate the number of test executions per month, you will need the following inputs:

  • Number of Locations (Probes)
  • Number of Targets (Tests)
  • Duration of Test (rounded to the nearest minute)
  • Frequency of Tests (in Minutes)
Using the above info, you can calculate the number of test executions per month by applying this formula: # of locations (probes) X number of targets (tests) X duration of test (rounded up to the nearest minute) X [43,200 total minutes in a month / frequency of tests in minutes)

Do you provide discounts for higher usage volumes?

Yes, we provide volume discounts for enterprise-scale usage. Contact us for more details.

FAQ

What is a virtual user hour (VUh)?

VUh is calculated by using the following formula:

(Maximum number of VUs x test execution duration in minutes) / 60 minutes = VUh

If your test ramps up to a maximum of 100 VUs and runs for 10 minutes, the test run will use 16.67 VUh ((100 VUs x 10 minutes)/60 = 16.67 VUh).

Note that VU hours are calculated based on the period that the test run executes, not the preconfigured test duration. If the test duration is 2 or more hours but runs for only 30.01 minutes, then the subscription will be charged for the test-execution period rounded up to the next minute (in this case, 31 minutes).

The minimum for a test is 1 VUh. Find additional details here.

What limitations are there in the free tier?

Both the free tier and free trial period are limited to 500 VUh / month.

How can I calculate how many VUH my tests will consume?

With our public calculator, you can model your testing workload and estimate the monthly and yearly consumption of VUH.

Do you provide discounts for higher usage volumes?

Yes, we provide volume discounts for enterprise-scale usage. Contact us for more details.

FAQ

What is a Grafana active user?

Any user that logs into Grafana during the billing month is considered an active user.

Does the Cloud Pro Pay As You Go plan include Enterprise plugins?

Enterprise plugins are a paid add-on for the Cloud Pro Pay As You Go Plan that is priced at $55 per active user per month. By default, your account will have Grafana without Enterprise plugins, which is priced at $8 per active user per month.

Do you provide discounts for higher usage volumes?

Yes, we provide volume discounts for enterprise-scale usage. Contact us for more details.

FAQ

What is an IRM active user?

An active IRM user is defined as a user who is included in OnCall schedules or escalation chains or performs any of the following actions:

  • Changed the status of an alert group or OnCall configuration
  • Received a page or paged another user
  • Created, edited, or updated an incident
